context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda stuck on the climate action plan prospectus rn. idk how to turn my city research into 2 actual policy components.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to reach out! Before we dig in — what city did you choose, and what are the main vulnerabilities or climate stressors you identified in your research so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very start of the task and feeling stuck. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment for them, I should ask a grounding question to find out what they already have (their SWOT analysis, city research, vulnerabilities) so I can help them build from their existing work. This is a natural Socratic starting point - get them to articulate what they know before we figure out what to do with it.

exchanges.student: it’s miami, but tbh i have too many notes and none of it feels usable. mostly sea level rise, flooding, heat, and like inequality stuff i guess.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong foundation — those are exactly the kinds of vulnerabilities the assignment wants you to work with. So here's a first step: if you had to pick just two of those issues to focus on, which feel most connected to each other or most urgent for Miami? Don't overthink it — what stands out to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has solid material - sea level rise, flooding, heat, and inequality are all highly relevant to Miami and to the assignment's focus areas (environmental justice, vulnerabilities, stressors). I should affirm this briefly but push them to start narrowing down rather than overwhelming them further. A targeted Socratic question asking them to pick two themes will help them self-select their two components organically.

exchanges.student: idk maybe flooding + heat, but now i’m confused bc that still feels way too broad. ngl can u just explain what a “policy component” even is in this assignment?
exchanges.tutor: Good question — but rather than me defining it, look at the Component Structure section of the assignment. It lists specific sections each component needs. What do you notice about how those sections are organized — what does each one seem to be asking you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for clarification on what a policy component is - this is a legitimate academic question I can help with Socratically rather than just defining it for them. I should point them back to the assignment structure and ask them what they notice about the required sections, rather than explaining it myself. This keeps them engaged in discovery.

exchanges.student: i see context/opportunity/policy rec/EJ/interactions, but that somehow made me more lost lol. that angle didn’t work; can i get the super basic version fr?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — think of it this way: a component is basically a mini-argument. You're saying 'here's a problem Miami has, here's a specific action the city could take, and here's who it affects and what else it connects to.' Does that framing make the sections feel a bit more logical?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking for a direct explanation. The rules allow me to be less Socratic when a student struggles, but I still cannot just hand them the answer. I can offer a gentle reframe using a concrete analogy to help them see the structure more intuitively, without writing their component for them. I'll keep it very short and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: kinda, but i still feel like i could write literally anything and call it that. can u give me a broad example for miami so i can see the shape?
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that — even a broad example for Miami could end up shaping your work too much, and that's your job! But let's build it yourself: you said flooding is one focus. What is one specific thing Miami is currently doing *or failing to do* about flooding that frustrates you or stands out from your research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an example for their city, which would essentially be doing part of the assignment for them. I need to hold firm on not providing content, even a 'broad' example, since that crosses into giving a partial solution. Instead, I should redirect them to use what they've already identified - flooding and heat - and ask them a question that helps them build the 'shape' themselves by thinking about one concrete piece of it.

exchanges.student: uh maybe drainage/infrastructure? like roads flood and some neighborhoods get helped faster than others. tbh can u just give me a long list of possible component ideas so i can pick?
exchanges.tutor: I won't make that list — that's your research to draw from! But notice what you just said: unequal drainage response across neighborhoods. That's not vague at all — that's a context, an EJ implication, and a problem all in one. What specific policy action could a city take to address that inequality?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the ideation work for them by asking for a list of component ideas. I need to decline this clearly as it crosses into doing the assignment for them. However, what they just said about drainage and unequal neighborhood response is actually excellent raw material - it hits infrastructure, flooding, AND environmental justice all at once. I should point that out and push them to develop it further themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe prioritize upgrades in vulnerable areas first, but i’m still not seeing how to stretch that into 200-300 words rn. give me the general formula maybe?
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think — 'prioritize upgrades in vulnerable areas' is already a real policy recommendation. The length will come from developing each section. Here's a question to push you further: how might better drainage infrastructure in vulnerable neighborhoods connect to *other* climate challenges Miami faces, like heat or public health?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they've identified a concrete policy idea. Now they're worried about length, which is a common anxiety. I shouldn't give them a formula as that would essentially scaffold the writing for them. Instead, I should point out that they already have multiple sections to fill (context, opportunity, policy rec, EJ, interactions) and that length will come naturally from developing each one. A targeted question about the 'interactions' section might help them see how depth grows organically.

exchanges.student: i guess less standing water and maybe safer streets, but tbh i’m still spinning. can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template i can copy?
exchanges.tutor: No template from me — that's your writing to do! But honestly, you have everything you need for your first component. Stop planning and just write 2-3 rough sentences for the Context section right now: what's the flooding situation in Miami and why does inequality make it worse? Share what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fill-in-the-blank template, which would essentially be doing the writing scaffolding for them - this violates the rule about not providing partial solutions or suggesting language. I need to decline clearly but without being harsh. I should also notice that we've been circling the same anxiety about getting started for several exchanges. The student actually has enough to start writing - flooding component with drainage, EJ angle, and an interactions link to heat. I should push them to just start writing a rough draft of the Context section and stop planning.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: miami has frequent flooding from sea level rise and heavy rain, and low-income areas don’t get upgrades as fast. also ppl there face more disruption and risk, but idk if that’s too obvious.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start for your Context — it identifies the problem and the equity dimension clearly. 'Obvious' doesn't mean wrong; it means you understand the issue. Now move to the Opportunity section: given that context, what specific opening or moment exists right now that makes action on this possible in Miami?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually written something solid for their Context section. I should affirm this genuinely without grading it, and push them forward to the next section rather than letting them second-guess what they have. The 'too obvious' concern is a distraction - the content is relevant and appropriate. I should move them along to build momentum.

exchanges.student: idk rn i’m blanking again lol, and the question-answer thing is making me more stuck. can u just tell me a simple opportunity i could use?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but giving you the content isn't my role. Think about it this way: has Miami received any federal funding, passed any local legislation, or faced any recent extreme weather events that created political pressure to act? Anything from your research come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking me to give them content directly. I cannot do this. However, I can acknowledge the frustration and offer a small redirecting hint that points them toward their own research without giving them the answer. I can remind them that 'opportunity' often comes from existing policy momentum, funding, or political will - and ask them to think about what they already know about Miami's situation. This is a moment to be encouraging but firm.
